“t-SNE is a statistical method for visualizing high-dimensional data by giving each datapoint a location in a two or three-dimensional map.”
What t-SNE is and why it matters
: each sample has dozens, hundreds, or thousands of features (e.g., gene expression, image pixels, word embeddings). Humans can’t directly visualize that.

For most modern single-cell and large-scale workflows,

Before t-SNE was introduced by van der Maaten and Hinton in 2008, the standard approach to visualising high-dimensional data was Principal Component Analysis (PCA). PCA is fast and interpretable, but it is linear — it can only represent structure along the directions of maximum variance. Many real-world datasets have intrinsic structure that lies on a low-dimensional nonlinear manifold embedded in a high-dimensional space. A 2D projection via PCA will collapse that manifold structure into a smear, showing clusters that are actually very similar and hiding clusters that are genuinely distinct.

t-SNE changed the field of exploratory analysis because it reliably reveals cluster structure that PCA misses. When researchers first applied t-SNE to MNIST digit embeddings, they saw 10 clean clusters — one per digit — with almost no overlap. When researchers applied it to single-cell RNA sequencing data, they could distinguish cell types that were indistinguishable in PCA plots. This made t-SNE the standard visualisation tool in bioinformatics and deep learning interpretability for nearly a decade.

Even in the context of generative AI, t-SNE has a role: visualising embedding spaces helps engineers understand whether a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system is grouping semantically similar documents correctly, whether a fine-tuning dataset has the right balance of examples, and whether a model's latent representations are forming meaningful clusters around topics or domains.
Captures curved manifolds and complex topologies that PCA's linear projection collapses.
Points that cluster in the 2D plot are genuinely similar in the high-dimensional space. Analysts can inspect and label clusters directly.
Gene expression, image pixels, word embeddings, audio features — any numeric feature vector can be reduced with t-SNE.
t-SNE is unsupervised. You can colour points by label after the fact, but the algorithm does not use labels. This makes it useful for discovering structure you didn't know to look for.
interface, making it trivial to integrate into any Python data analysis workflow.
t-SNE converts pairwise Euclidean distances in high-dimensional space into conditional probabilities using a Gaussian kernel. The conditional probability
as its neighbor if neighbors were chosen in proportion to a Gaussian centered at
is set individually for each point by binary search to achieve the target perplexity. Perplexity is defined as:
= (p(j|i) + p(i|j)) / 2n. In the low-dimensional space, similarity is computed using a Student t-distribution with one degree of freedom (a Cauchy distribution):

The t-distribution's heavy tails are the key design decision. In 2D, all points must fit in a confined space — there is geometrically less room than in 1000D. If a Gaussian were used in 2D, moderately dissimilar points would be forced too close together (the 'crowding problem'). The t-distribution's heavier tails allow the algorithm to place dissimilar points further apart without compromising the tightness of similar-point clusters.
The positions in 2D (the Y matrix) are optimised by minimising the KL divergence between P and Q using gradient descent:

has an efficient closed form that separates attractive forces (between similar points) and repulsive forces (between dissimilar points). The optimization runs for typically 1000 iterations with momentum and learning rate schedules to avoid poor local minima.

attrs.alt: t-SNE algorithm pipeline: from high-dim data through Gaussian similarity, Student-t mapping, and KL minimization to 2D output

content.text: Exploratory analysis of embedding spaces — visualising whether text, image, or audio embeddings form meaningful clusters.
Debugging a RAG or search system — do the document embeddings separate by topic as expected?
Bioinformatics — single-cell RNA expression, flow cytometry, mass cytometry data where cluster structure is the primary question.
Checking fine-tuning dataset balance — do examples of different classes form distinct or overlapping clusters?
Inference pipelines — t-SNE has no out-of-sample transform. You cannot embed a new point without recomputing the entire fit. Use UMAP or PCA for production dimensionality reduction.
Datasets larger than ~50,000 points — standard t-SNE is O(n^2). Use Barnes-Hut approximation (
When global structure matters — inter-cluster distances in t-SNE 2D plots are not interpretable. If you need to know that cluster A is 'closer' to cluster B than cluster C, use UMAP or PCA.

# Full t-SNE workflow: reduce embeddings from a sentence transformer and visualise
# pip install scikit-learn sentence-transformers matplotlib numpy
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer


def tsne_visualise(
    texts: list[str],
    labels: list[str],
    perplexity: int = 30,
    n_iter: int = 1000,
    random_state: int = 42,
    output_path: str = "tsne_plot.png",
) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Embed texts, reduce to 2D with t-SNE, and plot coloured by label.
    Returns the 2D embedding matrix (n_samples, 2).
    """
    # 1. Get embeddings
    print("Encoding texts...")
    model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")  # 384-dim embeddings
    embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
    print(f"Embeddings shape: {embeddings.shape}")

    # 2. Optionally reduce with PCA first (speeds up t-SNE on high-dim embeddings)
    from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
    pca = PCA(n_components=50, random_state=random_state)
    embeddings_pca = pca.fit_transform(embeddings) if embeddings.shape[1] > 50 else embeddings
    print(f"After PCA: {embeddings_pca.shape} (variance retained: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.2%})")

    # 3. Run t-SNE
    print(f"Running t-SNE (perplexity={perplexity}, n_iter={n_iter})...")
    tsne = TSNE(
        n_components=2,
        perplexity=perplexity,
        n_iter=n_iter,
        learning_rate="auto",
        init="pca",           # PCA init is more stable than random
        random_state=random_state,
        verbose=0,
    )
    embedding_2d = tsne.fit_transform(embeddings_pca)

    # 4. Plot
    le = LabelEncoder()
    label_ids = le.fit_transform(labels)
    cmap = plt.cm.get_cmap("tab10", len(le.classes_))

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
    for cls_id, cls_name in enumerate(le.classes_):
        mask = label_ids == cls_id
        ax.scatter(
            embedding_2d[mask, 0], embedding_2d[mask, 1],
            c=[cmap(cls_id)], label=cls_name, s=30, alpha=0.7,
        )
    ax.legend(title="Category", bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc="upper left")
    ax.set_title(f"t-SNE (perplexity={perplexity})")
    ax.set_xlabel("t-SNE dim 1")
    ax.set_ylabel("t-SNE dim 2")
    ax.tick_params(labelbottom=False, labelleft=False)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(output_path, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.close()
    print(f"Plot saved to {output_path}")

    return embedding_2d


# Example usage with synthetic data:
TEXTS = [
    # Technology
    "machine learning algorithms", "neural network architecture", "deep learning models",
    "transformer attention mechanism", "convolutional layers",
    # Biology
    "cell membrane structure", "DNA replication process", "protein folding dynamics",
    "gene expression regulation", "mitochondrial function",
    # Finance
    "stock market volatility", "portfolio optimization", "risk management strategies",
    "derivative pricing models", "interest rate dynamics",
]
LABELS = ["Tech"] * 5 + ["Biology"] * 5 + ["Finance"] * 5

# embedding_2d = tsne_visualise(TEXTS, LABELS, perplexity=5)  # small dataset needs low perplexity
# Expected: three distinct clusters in the 2D plot
Real-World Example — Visualising RAG Document Clusters

A team building a RAG system wants to verify that their document embeddings are semantically coherent — documents about the same topic should cluster together in embedding space, and retrieval should pull from the right cluster for a given query. t-SNE provides a visual sanity check before and after embedding model selection.

# Visualise a RAG document index to check embedding quality
# pip install faiss-cpu sentence-transformers scikit-learn matplotlib
import numpy as np
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


def visualise_rag_index(
    documents: list[str],
    categories: list[str],
    model_name: str = "BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5",
) -> None:
    """Embed documents and t-SNE plot to check if categories cluster cleanly."""
    encoder = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
    vecs = encoder.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True, show_progress_bar=False)

    tsne = TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=min(30, len(documents) - 1), init="pca", random_state=0)
    coords = tsne.fit_transform(vecs)  # (n_docs, 2)

    unique_cats = sorted(set(categories))
    colors = plt.cm.tab20(np.linspace(0, 1, len(unique_cats)))
    cat_color = {cat: colors[i] for i, cat in enumerate(unique_cats)}

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
    for cat in unique_cats:
        idxs = [i for i, c in enumerate(categories) if c == cat]
        ax.scatter(coords[idxs, 0], coords[idxs, 1],
                   c=[cat_color[cat]], label=cat, s=60, alpha=0.8)
        # Label centroid
        cx, cy = coords[idxs, 0].mean(), coords[idxs, 1].mean()
        ax.text(cx, cy, cat, fontsize=9, fontweight="bold", ha="center", va="center",
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.2", fc="white", alpha=0.7))

    ax.set_title(f"RAG Index: {model_name}")
    ax.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1))
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("rag_index_tsne.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")

    # Quality metrics: within-cluster cohesion vs between-cluster separation
    from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
    from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
    labels = LabelEncoder().fit_transform(categories)
    score = silhouette_score(coords, labels)
    print(f"Silhouette score (2D t-SNE): {score:.3f}  (higher is better; >0.3 = good separation)")


# visualise_rag_index(documents, categories)
# If silhouette score is low, consider a different embedding model or chunking strategy

The spacing between clusters in a t-SNE plot is not meaningful. Two clusters that appear far apart may not be more dissimilar than two that appear close. Only intra-cluster local structure is trustworthy.
Using the wrong perplexity for the dataset size.
For a dataset of 100 points, perplexity=50 means each point considers almost all others as neighbors — the result will be a single blob. For n=100, use perplexity=5–15.
The default in older scikit-learn versions was 200 iterations, which often produces under-optimised layouts. Use at least 1000 (
) and check the KL divergence in verbose mode.
Using random init on large datasets.
Random initialisation is sensitive to the random seed and can produce very different layouts on repeated runs. Use
Running t-SNE directly on 768-dimensional embeddings is slow (O(n^2 * d) per iteration). Pre-reduce to 50 dimensions with PCA first — this retains most variance and speeds up t-SNE by 10–15x.
Apply PCA to 50 components before t-SNE on any embedding with dimension > 50. Use
in t-SNE for deterministic, stable initialisation.

Run t-SNE with perplexity 5, 30, and 100 and compare the three plots. Structure that appears consistently across perplexity values is real; structure that appears only at one value may be an artifact.
t-SNE is non-deterministic with random init. Always specify
UMAP scales to millions of points and produces more stable, globally-consistent layouts. t-SNE's O(n log n) Barnes-Hut approximation is still slower and less consistent.
Exact t-SNE is O(n^2) per iteration. With n=10,000 and 1000 iterations, this is 10^11 operations — impractical. Use Barnes-Hut approximation (
) which is O(n log n) per iteration.
Storing pairwise distances requires O(n^2) memory. For n=50,000 at float32, that is 10 GB. Barnes-Hut builds a k-NN graph instead, reducing memory to O(n * k).
With Barnes-Hut, t-SNE handles ~50,000 points in a few minutes on a modern CPU. For >100,000 points, switch to UMAP or use
with FFT approximation which scales to millions.

The 2D t-SNE coordinates reveal the cluster structure of your data. If the underlying data is sensitive (medical records, private documents), publishing t-SNE plots can leak structural information about data distribution.
t-SNE results vary between runs without a fixed random seed. Always fix
and document the perplexity, learning rate, and n_iter used to reproduce a specific visualisation.

The most common reliability failure with t-SNE is drawing conclusions from cluster spacing or apparent topology. Stake the documentation: 'clusters in this plot represent local similarity; distances between clusters are not interpretable'.
method for visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D/3D while preserving local structure.
: Gaussian in high dimensions, t-distribution in low dimensions, minimizing KL divergence.
is faster, preserves more global structure, supports new data, and is often preferred in modern workflows, but t-SNE remains useful for fine-grain local granularity.
